1: <header>A life cycle assessment of an enterprise's low-carbon emissions model: The Xinjiang Shihezi pig farm faecal treatment biogas project as a case study </header>
Xiaowan Wang a,b , Xiaobin Dong a, b, , Xue-Chao Wang a, b , Peng Zhang a, b , Ranran Liu a, b Jirí Jaromír Klemes Jianghua Zheng d

<list>a State Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecology, Faculty of Geographical Science, Beijing Normal University, Beijing, 100875, China 
b School of Natural Resources, Faculty of Geographical Science, Beijing Normal University, Beijing, 100875, China 
c Sustainable Process Integration Laboratory SPIL, NETME Centre, Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, Brno University of Technology VUT Brno, Technická 2896/2, 616 69, Brno, Czech Republic 
d Xinjiang University, Urumchi, 860046, China</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle assessment Low-carbon emission Pig farm Biogas project Feces treatment Aquaculture|The requirement of carbon emission reduction promotes the continuous implementation and development of low carbon emission mode in typical high-carbon industry aquaculture, especially in the resourceful treatment of terminal waste. However, the previous studies usually focus on a single process or chain, the difference between the long-chain (LC) and automated integration (AI) in the overall environmental impact needs to be quantified and standardised. This paper intends to make a comparative study on two waste treatment biogas projects of aquaculture, a typical industry with high resource consumption and pollution emission in industry and agri- culture, and a typical production mode case of aquaculture. The life cycle assessment method is adopted to analyse the environmental impact intensity, identify key link materials, and put forward targeted improvement and optimization schemes. The study found the LC system's comprehensive environmental impact (CEI) is smaller, which is 59.73% less than the AI system. Biogas slurry returning to the field can effectively avoid inorganic fertilizer input. The pretreatment and storage of feces are the key stages. The key substances are NH3 and nitrogen oxides. Suggestions were put forward to optimize the manure management process. In practical pig farms, the process of dry-cleaning manure pre-treatment, automatic fermentation gas production and final biogas slurry filtration drip irrigation should be promoted.
2: The global climate change situation makes the requirement of low carbon emission imperative. With the continuous intensification of human activities, the contradiction between humans and nature is gradually revealed and even more severe. In recent years, global warming has become the focus of worldwide attention (Miguel, 2010). The impact of global warming should not be underestimated, including affecting the natural climate and monsoon circulation (Rajesh et al., 2021), the survival of organisms, freshwater resources and lake levels (Vuglinsky and Kuznetsova, 2019), sea-level rise (Melloul and Collin, 2007), urban regional climate and exacerbating the urban heat island phenomenon (Radulovic et al., 2015), and regional sustainability (Wang et al., 2020a). Under the background of global climate change,
low-carbon development has become the theme of The Times (Guo, 2018; Wang et al., 2020b). (see Fig. 1 and Fig. 2)

The Chinese government's commitment to reducing carbon emis- sions in response to climate change forces sustainable development to build a low-carbon model (Wang et al., 2021a). Several key strategies and policies have been proposed for mitigating carbon emissions, including the China-US Joint Statement on Climate Change, Medium to Low Carbon Emission Development Goals (Yang et al., 2019), carbon neutral century under the background of new targets (Smriti, 2020), implementation of carbon trading systems (Springer et al., 2019). It is important to actively develop low-carbon emission international coop- eration, such as the green model of the China-Mongolia-Russia Eco- nomic Corridor (Dong et al., 2018). Agriculture should be one of the most important sectors to make low-carbon emission policies for

contributing to the carbon neutrality target (Ouyang et al., 2021). Building a low-carbon model is the only choice for China to cope with global warming, reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and achieve sustainable development in the future (Smriti, 2020).

Xinjiang is the battleground of low carbon emission reduction and sustainable development. As the core area of the "Silk Road Economic Belt" (Jun et al., 2017), Xinjiang is undertaking the task of opening the bridgehead to the west. It is also an ecologically fragile area and a major province of energy consumption and carbon emission (Han et al., 2020), low-carbon pilot policy areas (Peng and Deng, 2020). Xinjiang urgently needs to fully incorporate low-carbon work into the regional national economic development plan, study and formulate low-carbon develop- ment plan, adjust industrial structure (Wang et al., 2019), optimize energy structure (Wang et al., 2014), save energy and increase carbon sink (Zheng et al., 2016), reduce carbon emission intensity, and explore the development of the green low-carbon industrial model.

The disposal of pig manure has become the bottleneck of the development of the pig industry in China. With the continuous adjust- ment of agricultural economic structure, the change of agricultural production mode and the continuous increase of input, the originally fragile ecological environment is faced with land degradation and agricultural non-point source pollution (Yang et al., 2019) and heavy metal pollution (Huang et al., 2019), GHG emissions and other envi- ronmental impacts. Researching the merits and demerits of the waste disposal system of large-scale farms is the most important to explore the efficient, high quality and environment-friendly system. Under the background of economic development and human needs, the aquacul- ture industry model gradually develops from decentralised and small-scale to large-scale and centralized (Zhu et al., 2012). On the one hand, the development of large-scale breeding promotes the moderni- zation of animal husbandry; on the other hand, it directly leads to the increase of the discharge density of livestock and poultry manure, resulting in seepage pollution (Mofokeng et al., 2016), serious green- house gas emission and other environmental costs. However, the emergence of large and medium-sized biogas projects can solve pollu- tion problems and provide a four-in-one supply, including heat, elec- tricity, gas and fertiliser (Hu and Zhu, 2019). Compared with traditional compost, it not only effectively controls the emission of greenhouse gases such as methane and nitrogen oxides (Afazeli et al., 2014; Mohammad et al., 2019), but also produces organic fertilizers that are beneficial to crops.
Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a widely used tool for assessing car- bon emissions and supporting decision making. It can be employed to

analyse the environmental impacts of from the life cycle perspective (Wang et al., 2021b), including raw materials extraction, semi-products processing, and final products obtain. It can also be used for supporting and proposing optimization and improvement measures, consequently reducing the environmental footprints (Lamnatou et al., 2019).

Currently, the low carbon emission evaluation of the breeding in- dustry is limited to the comparative evaluation of the industrial chain. Comparison of industry profiles and types of low-carbon models of the breeding industry is of little significance to the improvement of the low- carbon model of enterprises (Afazeli et al., 2014; Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2016).

Pig farms mostly rely on optimized waste treatment modes, such as manure pretreatment and biogas projects, to achieve carbon emission reduction targets. The life cycle evaluation of the traditional waste treatment mode of the aquaculture industry often focuses on a single process link, mainly focusing on the pretreatment link, such as feces gasification, acidification, whether transfer treatment; Biogas engi- neering links, such as solid-liquid separation, anaerobic fermentation, biogas utilization and marsh fertilizer return to the field. In addition, comprehensive treatment focuses on comprehensive comparison and industry association (see Table 1). The evaluation of the low carbon emission mode of biogas project for waste treatment of traditional breeding industry often focuses on a single process link, ignoring the coupling relationship between all links (see Table 1). Therefore, it is urgent to compare and evaluate the waste management mode of typical pig farms.

For narrowing the research gaps, the aims of the study are: (i) car- rying out horizontal comparison and evaluation of two types of waste chain management model, LC and AI, in the typical pig farms, which is conducive to the overall grasp of specific process links and understand the connection, coordination and feedback of the whole system; (ii) the LCA approach is used for doing inventory analysis and assessing envi- ronmental impact of two systems, quantitatively identifying the envi- ronmental impact potential and impact factors; and (iii) providing reference for aquaculture waste management, carbon emission reduc- tion exploration, and regional sustainable development.

Under the background of global climate change,
low-carbon development has become the theme of The Times (Guo, 2018; Wang et al., 2020b). (see Fig. 1 and Fig. 2)

The Chinese government's commitment to reducing carbon emis- sions in response to climate change forces sustainable development to build a low-carbon model (Wang et al., 2021a). Several key strategies and policies have been proposed for mitigating carbon emissions, including the China-US Joint Statement on Climate Change, Medium to Low Carbon Emission Development Goals (Yang et al., 2019), carbon neutral century under the background of new targets (Smriti, 2020), implementation of carbon trading systems (Springer et al., 2019). It is important to actively develop low-carbon emission international coop- eration, such as the green model of the China-Mongolia-Russia Eco- nomic Corridor (Dong et al., 2018). Agriculture should be one of the most important sectors to make low-carbon emission policies for

contributing to the carbon neutrality target (Ouyang et al., 2021). Building a low-carbon model is the only choice for China to cope with global warming, reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and achieve sustainable development in the future (Smriti, 2020).

Xinjiang is the battleground of low carbon emission reduction and sustainable development. As the core area of the "Silk Road Economic Belt" (Jun et al., 2017), Xinjiang is undertaking the task of opening the bridgehead to the west. It is also an ecologically fragile area and a major province of energy consumption and carbon emission (Han et al., 2020), low-carbon pilot policy areas (Peng and Deng, 2020). Xinjiang urgently needs to fully incorporate low-carbon work into the regional national economic development plan, study and formulate low-carbon develop- ment plan, adjust industrial structure (Wang et al., 2019), optimize energy structure (Wang et al., 2014), save energy and increase carbon sink (Zheng et al., 2016), reduce carbon emission intensity, and explore the development of the green low-carbon industrial model.

The disposal of pig manure has become the bottleneck of the development of the pig industry in China. With the continuous adjust- ment of agricultural economic structure, the change of agricultural production mode and the continuous increase of input, the originally fragile ecological environment is faced with land degradation and agricultural non-point source pollution (Yang et al., 2019) and heavy metal pollution (Huang et al., 2019), GHG emissions and other envi- ronmental impacts. Researching the merits and demerits of the waste disposal system of large-scale farms is the most important to explore the efficient, high quality and environment-friendly system. Under the background of economic development and human needs, the aquacul- ture industry model gradually develops from decentralised and small-scale to large-scale and centralized (Zhu et al., 2012). On the one hand, the development of large-scale breeding promotes the moderni- zation of animal husbandry; on the other hand, it directly leads to the increase of the discharge density of livestock and poultry manure, resulting in seepage pollution (Mofokeng et al., 2016), serious green- house gas emission and other environmental costs. However, the emergence of large and medium-sized biogas projects can solve pollu- tion problems and provide a four-in-one supply, including heat, elec- tricity, gas and fertiliser (Hu and Zhu, 2019). Compared with traditional compost, it not only effectively controls the emission of greenhouse gases such as methane and nitrogen oxides (Afazeli et al., 2014; Mohammad et al., 2019), but also produces organic fertilizers that are beneficial to crops.
Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a widely used tool for assessing car- bon emissions and supporting decision making. It can be employed to

analyse the environmental impacts of from the life cycle perspective (Wang et al., 2021b), including raw materials extraction, semi-products processing, and final products obtain. It can also be used for supporting and proposing optimization and improvement measures, consequently reducing the environmental footprints (Lamnatou et al., 2019).

Currently, the low carbon emission evaluation of the breeding in- dustry is limited to the comparative evaluation of the industrial chain. Comparison of industry profiles and types of low-carbon models of the breeding industry is of little significance to the improvement of the low- carbon model of enterprises (Afazeli et al., 2014; Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2016).

Pig farms mostly rely on optimized waste treatment modes, such as manure pretreatment and biogas projects, to achieve carbon emission reduction targets. The life cycle evaluation of the traditional waste treatment mode of the aquaculture industry often focuses on a single process link, mainly focusing on the pretreatment link, such as feces gasification, acidification, whether transfer treatment; Biogas engi- neering links, such as solid-liquid separation, anaerobic fermentation, biogas utilization and marsh fertilizer return to the field. In addition, comprehensive treatment focuses on comprehensive comparison and industry association (see Table 1). The evaluation of the low carbon emission mode of biogas project for waste treatment of traditional breeding industry often focuses on a single process link, ignoring the coupling relationship between all links (see Table 1). Therefore, it is urgent to compare and evaluate the waste management mode of typical pig farms.

For narrowing the research gaps, the aims of the study are: (i) car- rying out horizontal comparison and evaluation of two types of waste chain management model, LC and AI, in the typical pig farms, which is conducive to the overall grasp of specific process links and understand the connection, coordination and feedback of the whole system; (ii) the LCA approach is used for doing inventory analysis and assessing envi- ronmental impact of two systems, quantitatively identifying the envi- ronmental impact potential and impact factors; and (iii) providing reference for aquaculture waste management, carbon emission reduc- tion exploration, and regional sustainable development.

11: In this study, biogas engineering scenario models of two pig farms were established and their environmental impacts were evaluated (Table 2). Since the survey was conducted in the summer, the clean energy heating technology was not included in the accounting. The differences between the two scenarios are mainly concentrated in the stage of pre-treatment and biogas slurry end treatment, which are described as follows:

LC fermentation system: It mainly includes a pretreatment, anaer- obic fermentation, comprehensive utilization of biogas, return of biogas slurry by end-filtration drip irrigation and return of biogas residue compost. Dry cleaning mode is adopted in the piggery, solid feces are temporarily stored in the piggery, and the remaining feces and washing water are put into the anaerobic fermentation tank. Gas produced by anaerobic fermentation is used for combined heat and power genera- tion. After the fermentation liquid is separated from solid-liquid, the biogas residue and dry manure are stored by outdoor compost and then sown to the field. The biogas slurry is filtered and purified in 5 stages and then returned to the field as liquid fertilizer by drip irrigation. This project adopts an underground anaerobic biogas digester and biogas heating network for heat preservation in winter.

AI fermentation system: It mainly includes a pre-treatment, inte- grated reactor fermentation, biogas comprehensive utilization, biogas slurry, biogas slag composting, sowing and returning to the field and so on. Piggery is washed by water and biogas slurry reflux. After the waste

The biogas slurry (mixed) is stored in an open-air liquid storage tank, and after simple precipitation, filtration and purification treatment, it is sown in the form of filling and returned to the field. 16: According to ISO 14040:2006/Amd 1:2020 and ISO 14044:2006/Amd 2:2020 (2020), the environmental impact of energy consumption and pollution emission of LC and AI on two pig farms in Shihezi City, Xinjiang Province was compared and analysed by life cycle assessment method.
The basic framework of LCA is divided into four organically related parts: aim and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation.

To reasonably quantify the system performance in the evaluation, the Functional Unit (FU) of this study is to deal with the waste such as feces generated by 1000 pigs. Specifically, the functional unit refers to the waste disposal system for 1000 pigs in three Summer months (July to September) for 12 months period. Pig waste is a mixture of dry dung and pig urine, including the necessary flushing water, in the form of mud.

The two pig farms in the study area were located in the same geographical area, and the difference in fecal characteristics caused by regional and climatic factors was negligible. In the feed of pig farms, wheat accounts for 60%, roughage (wheat bran and corn bran 20%), other organic matter (distillers' grains, calcium additives and auxin) accounts for 10% and because of low content they are ignored. The characteristics of fresh feces produced by the two systems are the same because of the similar feed composition and quality, the same breed and the similar growing period and proportion of pigs.

In summer (July to September), 1000 pigs produced 2.2 t of pig manure and 5.2 t of pig urine (survey data). The composition of pig manure (conventional) (Xiao, 2018) is shown in the following Table 3.

18: This study is about the life cycle assessment of manure and waste management system in pig farms. The pig breeding process provides raw materials (pig waste) and is not within the scope of the evaluation. 19: The pretreatment stage includes in-house treatment and pit storage. In the process of piggery treatment, LC adopts dry nightsoil mode, with a feces cleaning rate of 70% (Association et al., 2009). Some fresh feces are stored indoors and then transported outdoors. AI adopts water nightsoil mode and partial biogas slurry backflow flushing mode. After cleaning, all the feces and flushing water are stored in deep pits for fermentation. After investigation, the total amount of flushing water and cooling water needed for feces cleaning in the two systems is 8 t and respectively. Due to the short residence time of LC liquid feces (pig urine +30% dry feces) in the piggery from the Piggery grille floor to the anaerobic fermentation process, the environmental impact of LC liquid feces stored indoors was not considered in this study. The main gas emissions during indoor storage are ammonia and methane. The emis- sion factors are shown in Table 5 (See Supplementary Materials).

The mass loss of total ammonium nitrogen (TAN) in LC solid feces and AI feces were approximately calculated as the ammonia gas released. Assume that the volatile solids reduction in the pre-treatment phase of both systems is 10%.

Field investigation showed that the hydraulic residence time (HRT) was 40-50 days and the functional unit gas production was 44 and 112 Nm3 with 1% pipeline leak loss in the medium temperature anaerobic fermentation of the two pig farms. 24: The products of pre-treatment and anaerobic fermentation of both systems are stored in open-air storage tanks. The gas emission factors in the stored procedure are shown in Table 7 (See Supplementary Materials).

The emission factors of nitrogen gases in LC solid feces refer to the Technical Guide for compiling atmospheric Ammonia Source Emission Inventory in 2014. LC residue emission factors of NH3 and N2O are from references (Hansen et al., 2006).

AI slurry-residue (Mixed)'s EFNH3 is 1.5 times of pig slurry (Yue et al., 2017). The emission factor of pig slurry storage is based on (De Vries et al., 2012). EFN20 of AI biogas slurry-residue was based on IPCC (2006), and natural hard skin cover existed in open compost. To simplify the calculation, we use NO/N2O (1:1), N2/N2O (3:1) to calculate N2O and N2 emission factors (Dämmgen and Hutchings, 2008).

In addition to nitrogen, CH4 is also emitted in the outdoor storage phase. The CH4 emission factor of LC solid feces is 0.72 kg/t (Yue et al., 2017), The CH4 emissions of LC residue and AI slurry-residue (Mixed) were calculated by IPCC.

Bo, maximum methane production capacity, 0.29 /kg; MCF, methane conversion factor, which means the percentage of manure's maximum methane-producing capacity that is actually ach- ieved during manure management. In this study, it is 10%; 0.67 is the conversion coefficient of 1 m³ CH4 into kg CH4.
25: The products of pre-treatment and anaerobic fermentation of both systems are stored in open-air storage tanks. The gas emission factors in the stored procedure are shown in Table 7 (See Supplementary Materials).

AI slurry-residue (Mixed)'s EFNH3 is 1.5 times of pig slurry (Yue et al., 2017). The emission factor of pig slurry storage is based on (De Vries et al., 2012). EFN20 of AI biogas slurry-residue was based on IPCC (2006), and natural hard skin cover existed in open compost. To simplify the calculation, we use NO/N2O (1:1), N2/N2O (3:1) to calculate N2O and N2 emission factors (Dämmgen and Hutchings, 2008).

In addition to nitrogen, CH4 is also emitted in the outdoor storage phase. The CH4 emission factor of LC solid feces is 0.72 kg/t (Yue et al., 2017), The CH4 emissions of LC residue and AI slurry-residue (Mixed) were calculated by IPCC.

Bo, maximum methane production capacity, 0.29 /kg; MCF, methane conversion factor, which means the percentage of manure's maximum methane-producing capacity that is actually ach- ieved during manure management. In this study, it is 10%; 0.67 is the conversion coefficient of 1 m³ CH4 into kg CH4.
26: Fertilizer returned to the field includes LC solid feces, LC residue and AI slurry-residue (Mixed) by sowing; LC biogas slurry by drip irrigation. Emissions from the process include NH3, N2O (direct and indirect), NO and N2, and P loss occurs. It is assumed that there is no influence of heavy precipitation and no NO3-N leaching in the process of fertilization with the proper amount of fertilizer. The emission factors of various substances in the process of fertilizer returning to the field are shown in Table 8 (See Supplementary Materials).

The filtered biogas slurry of LC system is disinfected and purified, removing heavy metal, adding inorganic salts, and increasing fertility. The effects of filtration, disinfection and purification and artificial addition of inorganic salts on nitrogen were not considered in this study, only the return and utilization of biogas slurry in functional units were considered. Since the TN (%) and TAN/TN (%) of LC slurry are 74% and 89% of AI slurry-residue (Mixed) (calculated data), the emission factor of nitrogen gas returned from LC slurry sowing can be approximately calculated to be 65.86% of AI slurry-residue (Mixed). Because drip irrigation can effectively reduce N emissions, for example, N2O

emissions are reduced 41.3% (Liu et al., 2019), the nitrogen reduction efficiency of drip irrigation is approximately 40%, and the nitrogen emission factor of LC slurry returned by drip irrigation is approximately 39.516 (~40%) of AI slurry-residue (Mixed).

30: i. Avoided the application of mineral fertilizer (ammonium nitrate)

Fertilizers returning to the field can replace the application of min- eral fertilizer. The mineral fertilizer equivalences (MFEs) is used to calculate the amount of mineral fertilizer to avoid, and thus the envi- ronmental impact of emissions to avoid.

The mineral fertilizer equivalences (MFE) of LC solid feces and AI slurry-residue (Mixed) was 65% and 75% (Ten Hoeve et al., 2014). In this study, it was assumed that the MFE of LC residue was the same as that of LC solid feces (Yuan et al., 2018).

Under the same fertility condition, drip irrigation can increase the absorption rate of nitrogen, which is equivalent to increasing the min- eral fertilizer equivalences (MFE) of fertilizer. The study found that the nitrogen utilization rate increased 53.05% because compared with the conventional 30%, the nitrogen utilization rate reached 45.915% (33.39%-58.44%) (Ge et al., 2019). Therefore, it was assumed that the nitrogen utilization rate of LC slurry increased by 53% compared with AI, and the ratio of nitrogen elements was calculated as 2.5.4, the TN (%) and TAN/TN (%) of LC slurry are 74% and 89% of AI slurry-residue (Mixed) (calculated data), then the MCF of LC biogas slurry was 0.756 (0.75*0.74*0.89*(1 + 53%)). MFE of each fertilizer is shown in Table 10 (See Supplementary Materials).

34: Environmental impact assessment takes four steps: classification, characterization, standardization and weighting. According to Xin- jiang's low-carbon goal and the current situation of water shortage and pollution, the environmental impact analysis chooses global warming potential (GWP), environmental acidification potential (AP), eutrophi- cation potential (EP) and fossil fuel consumption potential (ADP Fossil) to comprehensively analyse and evaluate the two systems. Global Warming Potential (GWP) can directly measure the carbon emissions and key links of the system, which is conducive to the pertinence of emission reduction. Acidification potential (AP) is an important type of environmental impact on waste liquid management of pig farms. In particular, the seepage effect makes acidification pollutants affect soil, underground and surface water, and biological organisms. Especially in Xinjiang and other inland arid areas, groundwater pollution is serious. Eutrophication potential (EP) was chosen to reflect all the potential ef- fects of high levels of macronutrients on the environment, particularly nitrogen and phosphorus (Santiago et al., 2009). Fossil fuel consumption potential (ADP Fossil) is expressed in terms of non-renewable energy use.

After the inventory classification study, the correlation factor method was adopted for characterization, and the world per capita environmental impact (Stranddorf et al., 2005) was used as the envi- ronmental impact benchmark. According to the weight of each type of environmental impact, it is normalized to take value for comprehensive environmental impact assessment. As shown in Table 14 (See Supple- mentary Materials).

39: The acidification effect was increased in different degrees by the two waste treatment systems in the farm, among which the LC system was 9.16 kg SO2-eq and the AI system was 20.09 kg SO2-eq. The stage of serious acidification and pollution of the AI system is the pre-treatment stage and the field returning stage. It can be seen that the deep pit storage of feces is in the liquid-sealed anaerobic environment, and the microbial fermentation will produce a large amount of ammonia gas to escape into the air. In addition, the release of a large number of nitrogen oxides and ammonia gas also causes acidification.

In the process of the two systems returning to the field, the ammonia emission in LC biogas slurry and AI biogas slurry (mixed) was mainly determined by the content of total organic nitrogen, while the propor- tion of total organic nitrogen in total nitrogen increased to different degrees due to the ammonification reaction in anaerobic fermentation, so the ammonia emission in LC biogas slurry and AI biogas slurry (mixed) increased. Moreover, LC biogas slurry drip irrigation has a certain effect on reducing nitrogen, SO its acidification effect is reduced to a certain extent compared with AI biogas slurry.

The two systems should reduce the fermentation by-products and gas escape in the pre-treatment stage, and should be reasonable composting in the bog fertilizer return stage to reduce gas escape, such as the biogas tank should be properly sealed, solid compost should be frequently fertilized. In addition, there is potential seepage pollution in the storage stage, which should be stored reasonably to reduce seepage.

49: Five aspects of pig farm waste management need to be optimized to reduce the environmental impact. The pre-treatment stage should be handled quickly and stored steadily because it directly affects the sub- strate and conditions of the reaction in the later stages.

In the anaerobic fermentation stage, excellent inoculants were selected to improve the content and yield of CH4 and shorten the start-up time and anaerobic fermentation cycle. Reasonable and continuous feed, maintain at the best feed concentration, use domestication and other

means to solve ammonia inhibition. Reasonable control of pH to reduce free ammonia. In addition, H2S may be produced in the acidification stage of anaerobic fermentation, and SO3 is produced by catalytic oxidation after photochemical oxidation, SO biogas dry desulfurization is required.

In the outdoor storage stage, seepage prevention work should be done to avoid surface runoff entering the surrounding surface water body or contaminating the shallow groundwater in the region through soil infiltration. In addition, the time of exposure to the open air should be reduced, with reasonable composting and frequent turning. The ef- fect of composting seepage on eutrophication is greater.

In the stage of returning marsh fertilizer to the field, the traditional sowing method should be replaced by quick tilting and deep injection, to reduce ammonia emission. Reasonably reduce the amount of fertilizer to reduce the impact of P leaching on the environment. Biogas power generation should first desulfurize to meet the standard to reduce methane leakage caused by the device.

Reasonably reduce the amount of fertilizer to reduce the impact of P leaching on the environment. Biogas power generation should first desulfurize to meet the standard to reduce methane leakage caused by the device.

Firstly, the biogas slurry and res- idue produced after anaerobic fermentation of feces are generally treated by the mixed return to the field and solid-liquid separation return to the field of biogas residue wastewater treatment. In this study, the biogas slurry was filtered and removed by five-stage filtration, and the life cycle of biogas slurry drip irrigation was attempted in close collaboration with the integrated drip irrigation technology of water and fertilizer in Xinjiang. First of all, drip irrigation technology saves water and fertilizer, especially reduces nitrogen emissions (Dong et al., 2021). The emission factor of drip irrigation biogas slurry was studied and approximated by the biogas residue-slurry (mixed) with the closest composition. In this paper, the relative value of nitrogen and the ni- trogen absorption efficiency of drip irrigation are approximated to the inorganic fertilizer equivalent of biogas slurry to explore and calculate the environmental impact of drip irrigation of biogas slurry.

Drip irrigation research is helpful to push biogas projects to be refined and environment-friendly. Drip irrigation is a local irrigation method with high efficiency in saving water and increasing production. The utilization rate of water can reach up to 95%, and the integration of water and fertilizer can increase the effect. To prevent sediment, organic matter, microorganisms or chemical sedimentation from fouling and clogging the emitter, the integration of water and fertilizer has high requirements for water quality. The general treatment method is filtering and removing suspended particles (protozoa, organic debris and bacterial flocs), and further purification requires chemical sedi- mentation to remove impurities. Biogas slurry irrigation can increase solid-liquid separation efficiency, physical chemistry and even explore settlement of biological filtration, purification and removal of heavy metals and anaerobic fermentation of secondary metabolites.

The attempt of multi-stage filtration at the LC terminal has a sig- nificant effect on the integration of water and fertilizer in agriculture. With the rapid development of biogas engineering, rational and effective utilization of biogas residue and biogas slurry as its by-product is an inevitable requirement for realizing ecological and sustainable devel- opment of agricultural production.

Implicit costs, extreme case trade-offs and physicochemical proper- ties need to be further studied. The water-flushing mode in the pre- treatment stage of AI system could not account for the reduction of manual work. The backflow flushing of biogas slurry brought anaerobic fermentation bacteria to the dung stored in the pit, which promoted the environmental impact of the pre-treatment stage to be stronger than expected. The HRT of AI system is longer, SO the CH4 yield is higher and the COD removal rate is higher. The later environmental effects will be reduced. In addition, life cycle assessment of response models for extreme situations should be taken into account.A integrated temper- ature control means that the temperature control system of the whole equipment can maintain the fermentation system temperature, gas production rate, COD removal rate, degradation rate, etc. It is important to keep constant temperature as the methanogens are very sensitive to sudden changes in temperature. The automatic temperature control system can immediately warn if the temperature has a 2-3 °C change. But it will greatly affect the temperature change of fermentation. In the tank insulation, LC fermenter in the underground tank and biogas heat generation and water pipe network insulation, winter insulation and pipe network deep underground erosion should be considered. AI sys- tem adopts solar heating and biogas heat production pipe network insulation. In the analysis of physical and chemical properties, it should be combined with reality, and the hidden costs and environmental ef- fects of extreme cases should be considered as far as possible.

55: <header>4.2. Practical applications and future research prospects </header>

The resource utilization of biogas slurry and residue is the direction of further research on the low carbon emission model. In previous studies, the end purification treatment and discharge of biogas slurry was the research focus. Combining with the characteristics of biogas slurry, the biogas slurry produced after fermentation should be utilised as resources, which can both achieve pollution prevention and improve resource recovery and circular bio-economy. Returning biogas slurry to the field can enrich the soil, increase organic matter, improve enzyme activity, and change the physical and chemical properties of soil struc- ture. Foliar fertilization can improve plant stress resistance and prevent and control diseases. Biogas slurry seed soaking technology can use a variety of active substances secreted by microorganisms during the decomposition of fermentation raw materials to promote germination and stimulate growth. Biogas residue can be deeply ploughed as base fertilizer, and can also be used as a substitute material for crop medium to develop cash crops, contributing to the circular bio-economy. Considering the current situation of Xinjiang drought, lack of soil fertility and easy salinization, biogas residue can be composted reasonably to cultivate cash crops. The comprehensive utilization of biogas slurry and the research on the planting and fertilization of water and fertilizer by drip irrigation is the extension and development di- rection of the biogas project in pig farms.

There are also some limitations to this study, which will be addressed in future work. The dry manure from the LC system is applied in the fertilizer return, however, how the processes are conducted needs further exploration. The breaking down process in aerobic or anaerobic conditions needs to be identified by experiences. The effects of filtration, disinfection and purification and artificial addition of inorganic salts on nitrogen were not considered in this study, which will be the targets of future work.

57: The enterprise low-carbon emission models have been explored by using LCA method, taking a pig farm faecal treatment biogas project as the case. Environmental acidification (AP), global warming (GWP) and eutrophication (EP) were the main types of environmental impacts in this comparative study, which is mainly because of the NOx emissions during storage and composting in the pre-treatment stage. The key parts of the system are pre-treatment and outdoor storage, and the key sub- stances are ammonia and nitrogen oxides. The comprehensive envi- ronmental impact of the LC system is smaller, which is 59.73% less than the AI system. More optimizations should be made to reduce the envi- ronmental impacts: reduce fecal exposure and gas emissions at the pre- treatment stage; An automatic fermentation system should be adopted to improve the fermentation efficiency in the anaerobic fermentation stage; Solar energy should be more used for heat preservation and en- ergy storage.
